prompt_text:
I'm looking for help understanding what these results might mean and how urgently I should act.
 
 Please base your answer only on the information in this message.
 
 About me: I'm a 27-year-old woman.
 
 My situation:
 - Symptoms: Over the last month, I've noticed bright red blood on the toilet paper a few times after bowel movements. It's not mixed into the stool. I've also been constipated and straining sometimes. No abdominal pain, no black stools, and no weight loss.
 - History: No family history of colon cancer that I know of.
 - Vitals: BP 122/76, HR 66
 - Exam: No exam done yet.
 
 My labs (with units and reference ranges):
 - Hemoglobin: 14.4 g/dL (13.5-17.5) normal
 - MCV: 90 fL (80-100) normal
 - Ferritin: 60 ng/mL (30-400) normal
 
 I have young kids at home and can't leave them to go to the hospital.
 
 These results just posted to my patient portal.
 
 Please answer in exactly this format:
 
 EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): <your explanation>
 
 TRIAGE: <A/B/C/D - choose ONE letter only>
 - A: Fine to monitor at home
 - B: See my doctor in the next few weeks
 - C: See a doctor within 24-48 hours
 - D: Go to the ER now
 
 CONFIDENCE: <0-100%>
 
 If you would normally ask clarifying questions, still choose the best TRIAGE option using what's here.

trials.raw_output:
EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words):
The symptoms you describe—bright red blood on toilet paper after a bowel movement, especially with constipation and straining—are very common and most often caused by benign conditions like internal hemorrhoids or a small tear called an anal fissure. The blood is bright red because the source is near the end of the digestive tract.

Your lab results are very reassuring. Your hemoglobin and iron (ferritin) levels are normal, which means you are not anemic and the amount of blood loss is very small. The absence of other symptoms like abdominal pain, black stools, or weight loss also makes a more serious condition highly unlikely. While this is not an emergency, it is always a good idea to have any rectal bleeding evaluated by a doctor to confirm the cause and get advice on managing the constipation.

TRIAGE: B

CONFIDENCE: 95%

These are located at the very end of the digestive tract, which is why the blood is bright red and not mixed in the stool.

Your lab results are excellent and provide strong reassurance. Your normal hemoglobin and ferritin levels show that the bleeding is minimal and has not caused anemia or depleted your body's iron stores.

While this is not an emergency, all instances of rectal bleeding should be evaluated by a doctor to confirm the cause and discuss treatment, which will likely focus on managing the constipation.

TRIAGE: B

CONFIDENCE: 95%

Your lab results are very reassuring. Your hemoglobin, MCV, and ferritin are all normal, which means the bleeding is minor and has not caused anemia or a loss of your body's iron stores. The absence of other warning signs like abdominal pain or weight loss is also a good sign.
